title: Configure and Initialize a Repository
summary: Set a repository-local author identity and turn an existing project folder into a repository on main.

sketchBlocks.title: Setting Up Your Git Identity
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
To start using Git effectively, it's crucial to configure your local Git identity. This identity is used to label your commits with your name and email address, ensuring that your contributions are properly attributed. 

Worked example:

```bash
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
```

In this example, we set the local Git identity for the current repository. The `user.name` and `user.email` are configured to ensure that all commits made in this repository are attributed to ZoeSkoul Learner. This configuration is stored in the `.git/config` file within the repository, and it overrides any global Git configuration for this specific repository.

sketchBlocks.title: Creating a New Git Repository
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Initializing a Git repository is the first step in tracking changes to your project. This process creates a `.git` directory in your project folder, which contains all the metadata and history for your repository.

Worked example:

```bash
git init
```

This command initializes a new Git repository in the current directory. After running this command, the directory becomes a Git repository, and you can start tracking changes. The initial branch is set to `main`, which is the default starting point for your project.

sketchBlocks.title: Checking the Status of Your Repository
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Once your repository is initialized, it's important to verify its status to understand what changes have been tracked. The `git status` command provides a summary of the current state of the working directory and the staging area.

Worked example:

```bash
git status
```

This command shows the current status of the repository, including any changes that have been staged for commit, changes that have not been staged, and untracked files. It's a crucial tool for understanding what changes are ready to be committed and what still needs attention.

sketchBlocks.title: Exploring the .git Directory
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `.git` directory is the heart of any Git repository. It contains all the metadata, configuration, and history of your project. Understanding its structure can help you troubleshoot issues and understand how Git manages your project.

Worked example:

```bash
ls -a .git
```

This command lists all the files and directories within the `.git` directory. Each file and folder serves a specific purpose, such as storing commit objects, configuration settings, and references to branches. While you should not manually edit these files, knowing their purpose can deepen your understanding of Git's inner workings.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of Version Control
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of version control systems like Git?
options: To track changes and collaborate on projects
To create backups on external servers
quizDraft.hint: Think about how version control helps manage changes.

help.concept: Version control systems track changes to files over time, allowing you to revert to previous versions.
help.hint_1: Version control helps manage changes and collaboration.
help.hint_2: It allows you to track changes and revert to previous states.

quizDraft.title: Git Identity Configuration
quizDraft.prompt: Which command sets the local Git user name?
quizDraft.hint: Look for the command that configures user.name.

help.concept: The git config command is used to set configuration options for Git repositories.
help.hint_1: The command involves git config and user.name.
help.hint_2: It sets the user name for the local repository.

quizDraft.title: Order of Git Initialization Steps
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the steps to initialize a Git repository in the correct order.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the sequence of commands to set up a repository.

help.concept: Initializing a Git repository involves setting up the directory and configuring identity.
help.hint_1: Start with configuring the identity, then initialize the repository.
help.hint_2: The final step is to verify the repository status.

quizDraft.title: Git Command for Initialization
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the command to initialize a Git repository: `git [blank1]`.
quizDraft.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

quizDraft.title: Git Status Command
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the command to check the status of a Git repository: `git [blank1]`.
quizDraft.hint: The command starts with git and checks the repository the missing term.

help.concept: The git the missing term command shows the current state of the working directory and staging area.
help.hint_1: The command is a single word after git.
help.hint_2: It checks the repository the missing term.

quizDraft.title: Configure Local Git Identity
quizDraft.prompt: Set the local Git identity for a repository with the name 'Alex Developer' and email 'alex@developer.local'.

solutionFiles.content:
git config --local user.name "Alex Developer"
git config --local user.email "alex@developer.local"

quizDraft.title: Explore the .git Directory
quizDraft.prompt:
Configure a repository-only Git identity in `project_delta` using the name `ZoeSkoul Learner` and email `learner@zoeskoul.local`.

First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter. Enter project_delta, run each local Git configuration command, and press Enter after each one.
quizDraft.hint: Read the Git task "Explore the .git Directory" and identify the repository state you need to create.

help.concept: This Git exercise checks the repository state produced by "Explore the .git Directory".
help.hint_1: Use git status before and after each small change so you can see what moved between the working tree, staging area, and history.
help.hint_2: Match the requested file names, branch names, and commit purpose before clicking Check Answer.

quizDraft.starterCode:
# First type: bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
# Press Enter, then follow the task in the lesson prompt.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
cd project_delta
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
quizDraft.instructions:
Configure a repository-only Git identity in `project_delta` using the name `ZoeSkoul Learner` and email `learner@zoeskoul.local`.

First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter. Enter project_delta, run each local Git configuration command, and press Enter after each one.

starterFiles.content:
# First type: bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
# Press Enter, then follow the task in the lesson prompt.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- "project_delta"
mkdir -p -- "project_delta"
git -C "project_delta" init -q -b main

solutionFiles.content:
bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
cd project_delta
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- "project_delta"
mkdir -p -- "project_delta"
git -C "project_delta" init -q -b main

forbiddenCommands.message: Do not rewrite shared history in this Git practice task.

hiddenShellCheck.script: test "$(git -C project_delta config --local user.name)" = "ZoeSkoul Learner" && test "$(git -C project_delta config --local user.email)" = "learner@zoeskoul.local"
